Name: spectral_stability_mixed_precision
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Mixed Precision Training in Large Language Models
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference in mixed precision training for large language models. By leveraging PAC-Bayes frameworks and Jacobian-based layer-skipping, we can minimize hidden-state perturbation and enforce strict wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in mixed precision training and quantization, such as SNIP, EMQ, and AMP-ViT. However, our approach differs in that we focus on spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to derive strictly bounded-overhead protocols. We also incorporate Jacobian-based layer-skipping to minimize hidden-state perturbation.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel approach to mixed precision training in large language models, leveraging spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference. By incorporating Jacobian-based layer-skipping, we minimize hidden-state perturbation and enforce strict wall-clock budgets. Our approach has the potential to significantly improve the efficiency and accuracy of large language models, while maintaining rigorous theoretical guarantees.
Experiments: We will conduct experiments on large language models, such as BERT and RoBERTa, to evaluate the effectiveness of our approach. We will compare our results to state-of-the-art baselines, including SNIP, EMQ, and AMP-ViT. Our evaluation metrics will include accuracy, FLOPs, and latency.
Risk Factors and Limitations: One potential risk factor is the complexity of implementing spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks in practice. Additionally, our approach may require significant computational resources to derive the bounded-overhead protocols. However, we believe that the potential benefits of our approach, including improved efficiency and accuracy, outweigh these risks.
